GitHub
● Fundamentals · Learn by Level · Interactive Demos

Shader Fundamentals by Skill Level

Audience: Beginner → Advanced
Mode: Interactive SVG demos
Approach: One concept per demo
🌱 Beginner
Interpolation — Lerp vs Smoothstep vs Step
Interactive — drag the slider
01Lerp vs Smoothstep vs Step
t = 0.50step = 1lerp = 0.50smoothstep = 0.50
STEP
step(0.5, t)
—
LERP
lerp(0, 1, t)
—
SMOOTHSTEP
smoothstep(0, 1, t)
—
t 0.500
Three interpolants that look similar on paper behave very differently at the edges. Drag the marker to see when each produces a hard seam, a linear ramp, or a smooth falloff.
When to reach for each
→step(edge, x) — binary cutoff. Use for hard masks, opacity cutouts, or debug visualizations.
→lerp(a, b, t) — straight-line blend. The workhorse for fades, blends, and mixing two textures.
→smoothstep(e0, e1, x) — S-curve between two edges. Use for feathered masks, soft transitions, anti-aliased edges.
See smoothstep in a real shader
06 Noise fields (Value / Worley / fBm) · smoothstep drives the colour ramp on all three
Common beginner mistakes
✗Hard seams everywhere. step() leaves aliased edges. For anti-aliased masks, smoothstep(x-d, x+d, t) gives you a filtered edge with width 2d.
✗Using lerp as a mask. lerp(a, b, t) is not clamped — if t > 1 you overshoot. Use saturate(t) first, or reach for smoothstep which clamps for you.
✗Smoothstep argument order. HLSL is smoothstep(edge0, edge1, x) — edges first, then the value. GLSL matches. Getting it backwards silently gives a flat zero.
Depth Fade / Soft Particles
Interactive — toggle hard vs soft, drag fade distance
B2Depth Fade / Soft Particles
HARD EDGEdepth diff —opacity 1.00
A particle quad intersecting scene geometry. Toggle between hard-clip and soft-fade modes, then drag the fade distance slider to see how the contact edge changes.
FADE 30
How depth fade works in UE5
→The Depth Fade material node samples SceneDepth and compares it to the particle's own depth. The difference drives a 0→1 mask.
→Pass the mask through smoothstep(0, FadeDistance, diff) for a feathered edge, or step(0, diff) for hard-clip.
→In Niagara, pair with Sprite Facing and make sure Translucency Sort Priority is set, or sort artifacts can swallow the fade.
Common mistakes
✗Fade distance too large. A fade zone larger than the particle radius makes the particle disappear before it fully intersects geometry.
✗Depth Fade on opaque materials. The node only works in translucent/additive blend modes; on opaque it silently returns 1.
✗Using PixelDepth instead of SceneDepth. PixelDepth is the fragment's own depth — you need SceneDepth (the background) for the comparison.
Sub-UV Flipbook Timing
Interactive — adjust FPS and toggle frame interpolation
B3Sub-UV Flipbook Timing
FRAME 1 / 16FPS 8INTERP OFF
A 4×4 atlas grid ticks through frames at the chosen FPS. Toggle linear interpolation to see how the ghost overlay smooths the transition — and why low-FPS atlases pop without it.
FPS 8
Sub-UV in UE5 materials
→Use the SubUV texture sampler or the SubUV Motion Blur node in the material to handle atlas tiling automatically.
→Drive the frame index from a Niagara SubImageIndex parameter, or compute it as Time * FPS % FrameCount in HLSL.
→Linear interpolation (SubUV Blend Enabled) blends two consecutive frames. Costs one extra texture sample but eliminates popping at ≤ 24fps.
Common mistakes
✗Frame count mismatch. If the material's Sub Image Size (e.g. 4,4) doesn't match the atlas layout, frames wrap incorrectly or show seams.
✗Driving index from world time directly. Time * FPS is not particle-relative; two particles spawned 1 s apart will be in sync instead of staggered.
✗Interpolation on a flipbook with alpha. Linear blending two semi-transparent frames doubles coverage — use premultiplied alpha or disable blend.
Channel Packing (RGBA)
Interactive — click channels to isolate each mask
B4Channel Packing — RGBA as 4 Independent Masks
RGB COMPOSITER = roughnessG = heightB = AO● A = emissive
A single texture stores four unrelated grayscale masks in R, G, B, and A. Click the channel buttons to reveal each one. One texture sample, four uses — half your sampler budget.
How to read packed channels in HLSL
→Sample once: float4 packed = Texture2DSample(Tex, Smp, UV);
→Then unpack: packed.r = roughness, packed.g = height, packed.b = AO, packed.a = emissive mask.
→UE5 convention for ORM textures: R = Ambient Occlusion, G = Roughness, B = Metallic. Check your art pipeline — conventions vary.
Common mistakes
✗Packing data with different compression needs. BC4 compresses one channel optimally; BC7 handles correlated RGB. A roughness map packed with an RGB normal loses quality — keep normals separate.
✗Forgetting sRGB on packed textures. Packing maps (roughness, AO, height) should use Linear color space in the Texture Editor. sRGB gamma-corrects the data and breaks your values.
✗Treating alpha as free storage. Alpha adds memory overhead on some platforms (BC1 has no alpha; you'd need BC3). Audit compression before packing alpha.
🌿 Intermediate
UV Flow / Distortion
Interactive — drag distortion strength
I1UV Flow / Distortion
PANNER ONLYdistortion 0.00t = 0.00
A panning UV grid with sinusoidal distortion. Drag the strength slider to see how the warp grows — and notice the point where tiling seams become visible.
DISTORT 0.00
UV flow in UE5 materials
→The classic two-layer trick: sample a flow map texture (stored in RG as -1…+1 offsets), add to UV, then sample your detail texture twice at half-phase and blend.
→In HLSL: float2 flow = FlowTex.Sample(S, uv).rg * 2 - 1; then uv0 = uv + flow * frac(Time * Speed);
→The Panner node handles simple linear flow. For turbulent flow use the FlowMap material function shipped with the engine.
Common mistakes
✗Single-layer flow. Without the two half-phase layers, the texture snaps visibly when it wraps. Use two samples at frac(t) and frac(t + 0.5) and crossfade.
✗Flow strength too high. Beyond ~0.3 units the warp tears tiling textures. Clamp with saturate or scale the flow map's contribution.
✗Non-tiling flow map. The flow texture itself must tile, or the UV offsets will jump at the seam — use a seamlessly-tiled noise export.
Spawn Rate vs Burst
Interactive — play and compare continuous vs burst cost
I2Spawn Rate vs Burst
t = 0.0 srate 0 aliveburst 0 alive
Continuous rate emits particles every frame; burst fires all at once. Press PLAY to see how particle count diverges over time — and how burst pierces the frame budget in a single spike.
Rate vs burst in Niagara
→Spawn Rate emitter: particles enter the pool continuously. Steady CPU/GPU cost, predictable budget impact.
→Spawn Burst Instantaneous: all particles fire in one frame. Particle count is capped but frame-N has a spike.
→For explosions / one-shot FX, prefer burst — fewer live particles across the effect's lifetime. For ambient smoke / rain, use rate.
Common mistakes
✗High burst count on a looping emitter. If the emitter restarts, the burst fires every loop — turns into an unbounded rate spike.
✗Ignoring Lifetime vs Rate interaction. ActiveCount ≈ Rate × Lifetime. Doubling lifetime doubles GPU cost for a rate emitter — tune both.
✗Benchmarking burst FX at frame 0. The spike is invisible in average profiling; check GPU frame time in the Niagara Debugger on the burst frame.
Sort Order Artifacts
Interactive — toggle draw order to see popping
I3Translucent Sort Order Artifacts
BACK-TO-FRONT✓ CORRECT
Three overlapping translucent quads. Toggle draw order to see how back-to-front compositing produces correct blending — and how wrong order causes color popping and ghosting.
Sort order in UE5
→UE5 sorts translucent objects by TranslucencySortPriority (integer, set per-component) then by camera distance. Same priority → camera-distance sort.
→Niagara particle systems have a Sort Mode per-emitter: None, View Depth, View Distance, Custom Ascending/Descending.
→For overlapping emitters (e.g. smoke inside fire), set explicit priorities — the further emitter gets a lower priority integer.
Common mistakes
✗Forgetting sort on GPU emitters. GPU sort adds ALU cost; it's opt-in. Without it, GPU particles draw in spawn order, not depth order.
✗Mixing Additive and Translucent in one emitter. Additive ignores sort (it's commutative). If some quads are translucent and some additive, split them into separate emitters.
✗Per-particle priority via material. You cannot change sort key per-particle inside a material — sort granularity is per-emitter (or per-component for meshes).
float vs half Precision
Interactive — zoom in to see fp16 banding
I4float (fp32) vs half (fp16) Precision
0.000 → 1.000fp32 — smoothfp16 — 10-bit mantissa
The same gradient rendered at full 32-bit and quantized to 16-bit. Zoom in to reveal where fp16 step boundaries appear — this is the banding you see in dark gradients on mobile.
Precision in UE5 shaders
→float = fp32 (24-bit mantissa). Safe everywhere. Use for positions, depth, anything that varies over a wide range.
→half = fp16 (10-bit mantissa). ~3× cheaper on mobile/console ALU. Use for colors, UV offsets, normalised values in [0,1].
→UE5 Material nodes default to fp32 on desktop, fp16 on mobile unless you use Precision node overrides. Check Shader Complexity with mobile preview.
Common mistakes
✗Using half for world-space positions. fp16 range is ±65504. A position at 10 000 UU overflows — use float3 for anything in world space.
✗Banding in dark gradients. A 0→0.1 ramp only has ~100 representable fp16 steps vs ~1 600 in fp32. Dithering or a LUT can hide this on mobile.
✗Assuming desktop == mobile precision. PC GPUs promote half to float silently. Always validate in a mobile preview or on device — the banding may not appear on PC at all.
🌳 Advanced
